glossary.def: A model that predicts the current value as a weighted sum of its own past p values plus a white-noise shock: y_t = c + sum phi_i y_{t-i} + e_t. The coefficient of AR(1), phi, is the one-step persistence of the series; stationarity requires the roots of the characteristic polynomial to lie outside the unit circle.

A model that writes the current value as the current shock plus a weighted sum of the last q shocks: y_t = mu + e_t + sum theta_j e_{t-j}. It has finite memory, so it is always stationary and its autocorrelation function cuts off sharply after lag q.

The property that a series' statistical behavior (mean, variance, autocovariances) does not change over time. ARMA models require it; an AR process is stationary iff every root of its characteristic polynomial lies strictly outside the unit circle (for AR(1), |phi| < 1).

Subtracting consecutive values, Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} = (1-L) y_t, to remove a trend. A series that becomes stationary after d differences is integrated of order d, written I(d); the 'I' in ARIMA. Over-differencing injects spurious negative autocorrelation, so difference the minimum needed.
An ARMA(p,q) model fitted to a series after d differences: Phi(L)(1-L)^d y_t = c + Theta(L) e_t. Special cases include AR(p) = ARIMA(p,0,0), MA(q) = ARIMA(0,0,q), and the random walk = ARIMA(0,1,0).

Seasonal ARIMA, written SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s, which adds a second ARIMA acting at the seasonal lag s (12 for monthly-yearly, 7 for daily-weekly). The uppercase orders capture the season via seasonal differencing (1-L^s)^D and seasonal AR/MA terms; the airline model (0,1,1)(0,1,1)_12 is the canonical baseline.

The autocorrelation function (correlation between y_t and y_{t-k}) and the partial autocorrelation function (the same after removing the intervening lags). Their cutoff/tail-off patterns are the fingerprints used to identify orders: PACF cuts off for AR(p), ACF cuts off for MA(q), both tail off for ARMA.

The iterative identify -> estimate -> diagnose loop for building ARIMA models: make the series stationary and read the ACF/PACF to propose orders, fit by maximum likelihood, then check that residuals are indistinguishable from white noise (Ljung-Box). If not, revise the orders and repeat.

refs.cite: Box, G. E. P., Jenkins, G. M., Reinsel, G. C. & Ljung, G. M. (2015). Time Series Analysis: Forecasting and Control (5th ed.). Wiley.
Hyndman, R. J. & Athanasopoulos, G. (2021). Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed.). OTexts.
Akaike, H. (1974). A New Look at the Statistical Model Identification. IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control 19(6).
Ljung, G. M. & Box, G. E. P. (1978). On a Measure of Lack of Fit in Time Series Models. Biometrika 65(2).
Dickey, D. A. & Fuller, W. A. (1979). Distribution of the Estimators for Autoregressive Time Series with a Unit Root. JASA 74(366).
Hyndman, R. J. & Khandakar, Y. (2008). Automatic Time Series Forecasting: The forecast Package for R. Journal of Statistical Software 27(3).
